bytes.topic: Package Management — lock files, versioning, dependency auditing
bytes.title: Ruby Bundler — Lock Files, Version Constraints, and Dependency Auditing
bytes.body: Bundler's Gemfile.lock is your reproducibility guarantee — it pins every gem and its transitive dependencies to exact versions. Never gitignore it in applications (libraries are the exception). The difference between `bundle install` and `bundle update` matters: the former respects the lock file, the latter resolves fresh. Use pessimistic version constraints (`~> 2.1`) in your Gemfile to allow patch updates but block breaking minor changes. For auditing, `bundler-audit` checks your lock file against the Ruby Advisory Database — run `bundle audit check --update` in CI to catch known CVEs before they ship. `bundle outdated` gives a diff of what's stale. If you're on Ruby 3.x, `bundle exec` ensures every command runs against your locked dependency graph, not whatever gems are installed globally. For monorepos or multi-app setups, scope lock files per app and use `BUNDLE_GEMFILE` env var to point Bundler at the right manifest.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Gemfile
source 'https://rubygems.org'

gem 'rails', '~> 7.1'
gem 'pg',    '~> 1.5'
gem 'redis', '>= 4.0', '< 6.0'

group :development, :test do
  gem 'bundler-audit', require: false
end

# Terminal — audit in CI
# bundle audit check --update
# bundle outdated --strict

bytes.topic: Type Safety — static vs dynamic typing, type inference, generics across languages
bytes.title: Ruby Type Safety — Sorbet Signatures Without the Boilerplate
bytes.body:
Ruby is dynamically typed, but that doesn't mean you're flying blind. Sorbet brings gradual static typing via `sig` blocks that are checked at both runtime and statically with `srb tc`. Unlike generics in Java or Rust, Ruby leans on parameterized types through `T::Array[Integer]` and `T::Hash[Symbol, String]` — composable, readable, and enforced only where you opt in.

The `T.nilable` wrapper makes null safety explicit: `T.nilable(String)` forces callers to handle `nil` rather than discovering it in production. Combined with `T.must()`, you assert non-nil at a boundary and get a runtime raise if violated — better than a cryptic NoMethodError three frames deep.

For duck typing purists, `T::Struct` and interfaces via `T::Props` give you shape-based contracts without abandoning idiomatic Ruby. Sorbet's `# typed: strict` mode pushes you toward fully annotated files, while `# typed: false` lets legacy code coexist. The incremental adoption model is why Stripe shipped it across millions of Ruby lines without a rewrite.

bytes.topic: Memory Management — GC vs ownership model, stack vs heap, memory safety
bytes.title: Ruby's GC Internals — How MRI Manages Your Heap and What You Can Do About It
bytes.body:
Ruby's MRI runtime uses a tri-color, incremental, generational mark-and-sweep GC — no ownership model, no manual free(). Every object lives on the Ruby heap, except immediate values (Fixnum, Symbol, true, false, nil) which are encoded directly in the VALUE pointer, never heap-allocated.

The GC runs in two generations: eden (new objects) and old (surviving objects). Old-gen objects are promoted after surviving a GC cycle and collected less frequently. Compaction (GC.compact, Ruby 3.0+) defragments the heap and improves CoW friendliness in forked processes — critical for Puma or Unicorn servers.

Common memory leaks in Ruby aren't dangling pointers — they're live references: global hashes, memoized caches, and Proc closures holding large objects. Use ObjectSpace.each_object to audit live instances. Enable frozen_string_literal: true to intern strings and cut allocations significantly. Monitor GC.stat[:heap_live_slots] and major_gc_count in production to detect GC pressure before it hits latency.
bytes.code_snippet:
# frozen_string_literal: true

require 'objspace'

# Inspect live object counts by class
ObjectSpace.each_object(String).count.tap { |n| puts "Live strings: #{n}" }

# Compact the heap (Ruby 3.0+) — run after boot in forked servers
GC.compact

# Snapshot GC state
stats = GC.stat
puts "Heap live slots : #{stats[:heap_live_slots]}"
puts "Major GC count  : #{stats[:major_gc_count]}"
puts "Minor GC count  : #{stats[:minor_gc_count]}"

# Force a full GC cycle for benchmarking
GC.start(full_mark: true, immediate_sweep: true)

bytes.topic: Error Handling — exceptions vs Result types, error propagation patterns per language
bytes.title: Ruby Error Handling — Exceptions vs dry-monads Result Types
bytes.body:
Ruby's exception system is expressive but silent — `rescue StandardError` catches broadly, and errors propagate invisibly up the call stack. For predictable failure paths (user not found, invalid input), the `dry-monads` gem's `Result` monad makes failures explicit in the return type. `Success(value)` and `Failure(reason)` force callers to handle both branches at the call site.

The critical Ruby-specific rule: always inherit custom exceptions from `StandardError`, never from `Exception` — bare `rescue` only catches `StandardError` and below. Use exceptions for truly unexpected conditions (network timeout, disk full); use `Result` for domain failures.

`dry-monads`' `Do` notation lets you propagate failures with `yield`, mirroring Rust's `?` operator without nested `bind` chains. For existing exception-raising code, wrap it with `rescue => e; Failure(e.message)` to bridge both worlds. This pattern is especially clean in service objects and command patterns where error paths are treated as first-class return values, not invisible side exits.
bytes.code_snippet:
require 'dry/monads'
require 'dry/monads/do'

class UserService
  include Dry::Monads[:result]
  include Dry::Monads::Do.for(:call)

  def call(user_id)
    user  = yield find_user(user_id)
    yield validate_active(user)
    Success(user)
  end

  private

  def find_user(id)
    user = User.find_by(id: id)
    user ? Success(user) : Failure(:not_found)
  end

  def validate_active(user)
    user.active? ? Success(user) : Failure(:inactive)
  end
end

bytes.topic: Performance — benchmarking, profiling, runtime characteristics per language
bytes.title: Ruby Performance — benchmark-ips, stackprof, and YJIT Profiling
bytes.body: Ruby's stdlib `Benchmark` module measures wall time, which is noisy. The `benchmark-ips` gem measures iterations-per-second with a configurable warmup phase, giving statistically stable results. Use `x.compare!` to get a relative ratio between reports — essential when choosing between idioms like `select + map` vs `filter_map` (introduced in Ruby 2.7). For CPU profiling, `stackprof` is the go-to: it samples the call stack at a configurable interval and outputs a flamegraph-compatible dump with minimal overhead (~1–2%). Run it with `StackProf.run(mode: :cpu, interval: 1000)` and inspect with `stackprof --text`. For object allocation hotspots, `memory_profiler` tracks retained vs allocated objects per line. Finally, YJIT (enabled via `--yjit` since Ruby 3.1, on by default in 3.3) can yield 10–30% throughput gains on real Rails workloads. Validate gains with `RubyVM::YJIT.runtime_stats` to confirm exit reasons and code-region coverage before shipping to production.
bytes.code_snippet:
require 'benchmark/ips'

data = (1..10_000).to_a

Benchmark.ips do |x|
  x.config(warmup: 2, time: 5)

  x.report('select + map') { data.select(&:odd?).map { |n| n * 2 } }
  x.report('filter_map')   { data.filter_map { |n| n * 2 if n.odd? } }

  x.compare!
end

bytes.topic: Concurrency Model — async/await, goroutines, threads, actors — how each language handles it
bytes.title: Ruby's Concurrency Model — GVL, Fibers, and Ractors Explained
bytes.body:
Ruby's concurrency story is defined by the GVL (Global VM Lock) in MRI — only one thread executes Ruby bytecode at a time. Threads are real OS threads, but the GVL serializes CPU-bound work. The payoff comes with I/O: network calls, file reads, and `sleep` release the GVL, so a thread pool genuinely improves throughput in a Rails app under load.

Fibers are Ruby's cooperative coroutine primitive — lightweight, manually scheduled via `Fiber#resume` and `Fiber.yield`. They're the foundation of the `async` gem (by Samuel Williams), which layers a non-blocking event loop on top without new syntax. No `async`/`await` keywords; just Fibers managed by a reactor.

Ractors (Ruby 3.0+) unlock true parallelism. Each Ractor gets its own GVL, communicates via `send`/`take` message passing, and enforces isolated or frozen shared state. The trade-off is sharp: most gems aren't Ractor-safe yet, so adoption is constrained to greenfield CPU-parallel workloads.

The mental model: reach for threads on I/O-bound concurrency, the `async` gem for high-connection servers, and Ractors only when you can control the dependency surface.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Threads — I/O releases the GVL, so this runs in ~1s not 4s
threads = 4.times.map { Thread.new { sleep 1 } }
threads.each(&:join)

# Ractors — true CPU parallelism, isolated memory
workers = 4.times.map { |i| Ractor.new(i) { |n| n ** 2 } }
p workers.map(&:take)   # => [0, 1, 4, 9], runs in parallel

# Fibers — cooperative coroutines, single-threaded
counter = Fiber.new do
  n = 0
  loop { Fiber.yield(n += 1) }
end
p counter.resume   # => 1
p counter.resume   # => 2

bytes.topic: Runtime & Execution — compiled vs interpreted vs JIT, startup time, cold start
bytes.title: Ruby's Execution Model — MRI, JIT, and Why Cold Starts Still Sting
bytes.body:
Ruby (MRI) is interpreted: your `.rb` files are parsed into an AST, compiled to YARV bytecode, then executed by the YARV virtual machine — all at runtime, every run. There's no ahead-of-time binary. This means every `ruby script.rb` invocation pays the full parse-and-compile tax upfront, which is why Rails cold starts on Lambda or container-per-request setups can hit 800ms–2s before a single line of your business logic runs.

Ruby 3.1 introduced YJIT (a Rust-rewritten, in-process JIT), enabled via `--yjit` or `RUBY_YJIT_ENABLE=1`. YJIT compiles hot bytecode blocks to native machine code lazily — it doesn't eliminate cold start, but it cuts steady-state throughput latency by 15–30% on real Rails workloads per Shopify benchmarks.

For serverless or CLI tools, cold start is the enemy. Strategies: use `bootsnap` to cache bytecode to disk, freeze string literals with `# frozen_string_literal: true` to reduce object allocation during boot, and measure with `time ruby -e 'require_relative "config/application"'` to isolate framework load time from request time.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Measure raw Ruby boot cost vs. your app load
# Run from Rails root

# 1. Bare Ruby startup
time ruby -e 'puts "boot done"'

# 2. With bootsnap cache (add to Gemfile: gem 'bootsnap')
BOOTSNAP_CACHE_DIR=tmp/cache ruby -r bootsnap/setup -e 'puts "boot done"'

# 3. Enable YJIT and confirm it's active
RUBY_YJIT_ENABLE=1 ruby --yjit -e 'puts RubyVM::YJIT.enabled?'

# 4. Check YJIT stats in a running process
if defined?(RubyVM::YJIT)
  pp RubyVM::YJIT.runtime_stats
end

bytes.topic: Standard Library — what's built in vs what needs a third-party library
bytes.title: Ruby's Standard Library — What You Already Have vs When You Need a Gem
bytes.body:
Ruby ships with a surprisingly capable stdlib that developers routinely overlook. `JSON`, `CSV`, `URI`, `Digest`, `Benchmark`, `Logger`, `Pathname`, `FileUtils`, `Set`, `OpenStruct`, and `Net::HTTP` are all zero-dependency. `require 'json'` gets you `JSON.parse` and `JSON.generate`. `require 'digest'` gives you SHA256 without openssl gems. `require 'benchmark'` lets you time blocks inline.

Where stdlib falls short: `Net::HTTP` is functional but verbose — reach for `faraday` or `httpx` for real HTTP clients. `CSV` handles parsing fine but not large streaming datasets efficiently. `Logger` works for simple logging; use `semantic_logger` or `lograge` in production Rails. There's no built-in async primitive beyond `Thread` — `concurrent-ruby` or `async` gem fills that gap. No JSON schema validation, no JWT, no background jobs.

The practical rule: before `bundle add`, run `ri ClassName` or check `ruby-doc.org`. Gems like `activesupport` re-implement things already in stdlib (`Set`, `Pathname`) and add load overhead. Know what 2.x vs 3.x stdlib added — `Data.define` (3.2) and `Fiber::Scheduler` (3.0) are underused built-ins.
bytes.code_snippet:
require 'json'
require 'digest'
require 'benchmark'
require 'set'

# All stdlib — no Gemfile entries needed
payload = JSON.parse('{"user": "alice"}')
hash    = Digest::SHA256.hexdigest(payload['user'])
uniques = Set.new(['a', 'b', 'a'])  # => #<Set: {"a", "b"}>

Benchmark.bm do |x|
  x.report('sha256 x1000:') { 1000.times { Digest::SHA256.hexdigest('hello') } }
end

# This DOES need a gem (Net::HTTP is too verbose for real use)
# gem 'httpx'
# HTTPX.get('https://api.example.com/data')

bytes.topic: Testing — unit testing philosophy and test runner per language
bytes.title: Ruby Unit Testing — RSpec vs Minitest and When to Use Each
bytes.body: Ruby ships with Minitest in its standard library — no gem required. It's fast, minimal, and uses plain Ruby classes and methods. RSpec, the dominant alternative, brings a DSL built around `describe`, `context`, and `it` blocks with `expect(...).to` matchers. The philosophical split matters: Minitest encourages tests as code; RSpec encourages tests as documentation. For libraries and gems, Minitest is preferred — zero dependencies, readable diffs, and `assert_equal expected, actual` is explicit. For Rails apps, RSpec + FactoryBot + Shoulda Matchers is the de facto stack. Ruby's unit testing philosophy centers on isolation: use `Minitest::Mock` or RSpec's `double` to stub collaborators, never hit the DB in a unit test. Both runners support `before`/`after` hooks, parallelism via `parallel_tests`, and coverage via `simplecov`. The key Ruby-specific insight: modules mixed into objects make targeted unit testing easier — test the behavior the module adds without instantiating a full domain object. Prefer `subject { described_class.new }` in RSpec and `let` for lazy-evaluated fixtures.
bytes.code_snippet:
# rspec example — testing a Ruby module in isolation
RSpec.describe Discountable do
  let(:product) do
    obj = Object.new
    obj.extend(Discountable)
    allow(obj).to receive(:base_price).and_return(100)
    obj
  end

  describe '#discounted_price' do
    it 'applies percentage discount correctly' do
      expect(product.discounted_price(20)).to eq(80)
    end
  end
end

bytes.topic: Security — memory safety, injection prevention, dependency vulnerability scanning
bytes.title: Ruby Security — Memory Safety, Injection Prevention, and Dependency Scanning
bytes.body: Ruby's managed runtime handles memory allocation and garbage collection, eliminating buffer overflows and use-after-free bugs common in C/C++. However, Ruby apps face injection risks at the application layer. ActiveRecord parameterized queries prevent SQL injection, but raw string interpolation in `where` clauses is still a trap developers fall into. For shell commands, avoid `system("#{user_input}")` — use the array form `system('ls', user_input)` which bypasses shell interpolation entirely. For YAML, disable unsafe deserialization: `YAML.safe_load(data)` instead of `YAML.load(data)`, which prior to Ruby 3.1 could execute arbitrary objects. On the dependency front, `bundle audit` (via the `bundler-audit` gem) scans your `Gemfile.lock` against the Ruby Advisory Database for known CVEs. Integrate it into CI to catch vulnerable gems before deployment. Also run `brakeman` — a static analysis tool that detects injection flaws, XSS, and mass assignment vulnerabilities specific to Rails codebases. Together, these tools cover the three primary Ruby security vectors: runtime injection, unsafe deserialization, and supply chain vulnerabilities.
bytes.code_snippet:
# BAD: shell injection risk
system("convert #{params[:filename]} output.png")

# GOOD: array form bypasses shell
system('convert', params[:filename], 'output.png')

# BAD: YAML arbitrary object instantiation
obj = YAML.load(user_yaml)  # dangerous pre-Ruby 3.1

# GOOD: safe subset only
obj = YAML.safe_load(user_yaml)

# Dependency audit in CI
# gem install bundler-audit brakeman
# bundle audit check --update
# brakeman -q --no-pager

bytes.topic: Code Quality — linting, formatting, language-specific idioms
bytes.title: Ruby Code Quality — RuboCop, Style Cops, and Idiomatic Patterns
bytes.body:
RuboCop is Ruby's de facto linter and formatter, enforcing the community style guide out of the box. Run `rubocop --autocorrect` to fix safe offenses automatically. Configure it via `.rubocop.yml` — inherit from `rubocop-rails`, `rubocop-rspec`, or `rubocop-performance` gems for domain-specific cops.

Beyond formatting, Ruby idioms matter. Prefer `map` over `each` with a push, use `tap` for fluent object setup, and lean on `then`/`yield_self` for pipeline-style transformations. Avoid `for` loops — `each` is the Ruby way. Use `frozen_string_literal: true` at the top of every file to freeze string literals and reduce allocations.

For method length, RuboCop's `Metrics/MethodLength` cop defaults to 10 lines — keep methods focused. Use `guard clauses` (early returns) instead of nested conditionals. `StandardRB` is a zero-config alternative that wraps RuboCop with opinionated, non-negotiable defaults — useful when teams waste time debating style over substance.
bytes.code_snippet:
# frozen_string_literal: true

# .rubocop.yml excerpt
# inherit_gem:
#   rubocop-rails: config/rails.yml
#   rubocop-performance: config/performance.yml

# Bad: mutation in loop
results = []
items.each { |i| results << i.process }

# Good: idiomatic map
results = items.map(&:process)

# Good: tap for setup, then for pipeline
user = User.new.tap { |u| u.role = :admin }
value = raw_input.then { |v| sanitize(v) }.then { |v| persist(v) }

bytes.topic: Deployment — binary size, containerization, runtime dependencies
bytes.title: Ruby Deployment — Shrinking Container Images and Taming Runtime Dependencies
bytes.body:
Ruby's default Docker images are notoriously bloated. The official `ruby:3.3` image ships at ~1GB due to build tools, dev headers, and documentation. Switch to `ruby:3.3-slim` and you drop to ~200MB immediately. For production, multi-stage builds let you compile native gems (e.g. `pg`, `nokogiri`) in a builder stage, then copy only `/usr/local/bundle` into a slim runtime image — no gcc, no make, no headers at runtime.

Bundler is your dependency boundary. Use `bundle config set --local without 'development test'` before `bundle install` in your Dockerfile to exclude non-production gems. Pair this with `BUNDLE_FROZEN=1` to prevent accidental gem resolution at startup.

For binary size, `bootsnap` caches YARV bytecode and reduces boot time by 50–70% without touching your image footprint. Store its cache in a tmpfs volume or warm it during the build step. If you're on JRuby, AOT compilation via `jrubyc` produces `.class` files you can ship without the source.

Finally, pin your Ruby version with `.ruby-version` and match it exactly in your `FROM` directive — version drift between development and container is the most common cause of native extension failures.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Multi-stage Dockerfile for a Rails/Rack app
FROM ruby:3.3-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y build-essential libpq-dev
COPY Gemfile Gemfile.lock ./
RUN bundle config set --local without 'development test' \
 && bundle install --jobs 4 --retry 3

FROM ruby:3.3-slim AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libpq5 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/bundle /usr/local/bundle
COPY . .
ENV BUNDLE_FROZEN=1 RAILS_ENV=production
CMD ["bundle", "exec", "puma", "-C", "config/puma.rb"]

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling — REPL, debugger, language server protocol support
bytes.title: Ruby Dev Tooling: debug gem, binding.irb, and Ruby LSP in Your Workflow
bytes.body:
Ruby 3.1+ ships with the `debug` gem as the official debugger, replacing the aging `byebug`. Drop `binding.break` anywhere in your code — it launches a full REPL session mid-execution with step, next, continue, and frame commands. Unlike byebug, it supports multithreading, remote debugging over Unix sockets, and a VS Code DAP integration via the `rdbg` binary.

For the REPL, IRB has been overhauled since Ruby 3.0 — it now supports multiline editing, syntax highlighting, autocomplete backed by RDoc, and `IRB::Irb#context` introspection. Use `binding.irb` to break into IRB inline during test runs or scripts.

On the LSP front, Shopify's `ruby-lsp` gem has become the de facto standard over Solargraph. It provides semantic highlighting, accurate go-to-definition via RBS type signatures, and a built-in test runner integration. Pair it with the `ruby-lsp-rails` addon for Rails-specific completions. Add `ruby-lsp` to your Gemfile's development group and configure VS Code's Ruby LSP extension — no extra config needed.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Gemfile (development group)
gem 'ruby-lsp'
gem 'ruby-lsp-rails'

# Drop into debugger mid-execution
def process_order(order)
  binding.break  # launches `rdbg` session here
  order.line_items.map { |item| item.price * item.quantity }.sum
end

# Or use IRB inline
def inspect_user(user)
  binding.irb   # full IRB REPL with local scope
end

# Run with debugger from CLI
# rdbg -e ruby my_script.rb

bytes.topic: Scalability — horizontal scaling characteristics, thread model implications
bytes.title: Ruby Horizontal Scaling — GVL Constraints and the Puma Cluster Model
bytes.body:
MRI Ruby's Global VM Lock (GVL) serializes bytecode execution across threads within a single process — only one thread runs Ruby code at a time. CPU-bound workloads gain nothing from adding threads; IO-bound tasks do, since the GVL is released during blocking IO (network calls, disk reads, `sleep`). This makes horizontal scaling the primary throughput lever.

Puma's cluster mode forks N worker processes, each owning its own GVL, enabling true parallelism across cores. Threads within each worker handle IO concurrency cheaply. The canonical production formula: `WEB_CONCURRENCY = vCPU count` workers, `RAILS_MAX_THREADS = 5` threads per worker. Connection pool size in `database.yml` must match thread count or you'll exhaust `ActiveRecord::Base.connection_pool`.

Ruby 3.x Ractors offer GVL-free parallelism in-process, but their strict object isolation — no shared mutable state across Ractor boundaries — limits adoption for typical Rails workloads. JRuby and TruffleRuby remove the GVL entirely, making thread-per-request viable, at the cost of native gem compatibility.

For most Rails deployments: replicate stateless containers horizontally, size Puma workers to CPU, and use threads purely for IO overlap — not CPU parallelism.
bytes.code_snippet:
# config/puma.rb
workers ENV.fetch("WEB_CONCURRENCY") { 2 }
threads_count = ENV.fetch("RAILS_MAX_THREADS") { 5 }
threads threads_count, threads_count

preload_app!

on_worker_boot do
  # Each forked worker needs its own DB connection pool
  ActiveRecord::Base.establish_connection if defined?(ActiveRecord)
end

bytes.topic: Ecosystem Maturity — library quality, community size, long-term viability
bytes.title: Ruby's Ecosystem Maturity — Signal-to-Noise Ratio That npm Envies
bytes.body:
RubyGems hosts over 170,000 gems, but Ruby's real strength is curation depth, not breadth. Core libraries like Sidekiq, Devise, Faraday, and RSpec have decade-long track records, semantic versioning discipline, and changelogs that actually document breaking changes. Bundler's lockfile model (`Gemfile.lock`) was the gold standard for reproducible builds before npm or Cargo existed.

Ruby 3.x has directly addressed the ecosystem's longest-standing liability — performance. YJIT (written in Rust, shipping by default in 3.2+) delivers 2–3× throughput improvements on Rails apps with zero code changes. Shopify, GitHub, and Stripe running Rails at scale are the strongest signal of long-term viability you can get.

Evaluate gem health before adopting: check `rubygems.org` for download velocity, open issue age, and last push date. The Ruby Toolbox (ruby-toolbox.com) ranks gems by category with GitHub metrics. A gem with 100M downloads, active commits, and a <30-day last release is safer than a 1-star npm package with 10M weekly downloads.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Gemfile — version pinning with ecosystem-mature gems
source 'https://rubygems.org'

gem 'rails',   '~> 7.1'
gem 'sidekiq', '~> 7.0'   # battle-tested background jobs since 2012
gem 'devise',  '~> 4.9'   # auth; 1B+ downloads
gem 'faraday', '~> 2.9'   # HTTP client with middleware stack

group :development, :test do
  gem 'rspec-rails', '~> 6.1'
  gem 'factory_bot_rails'
end

# Check installed gem metadata at runtime
puts Gem::Specification.find_by_name('sidekiq').version
puts Gem::Specification.find_by_name('sidekiq').homepage

bytes.topic: Language-specific — Node.js event loop, Python GIL, Go channels, Rust ownership, Elixir OTP
bytes.title: Ruby's GVL — Why Threads Don't Buy You True Parallelism (And What Does)
bytes.body:
MRI Ruby's Global VM Lock (GVL, formerly GIL) ensures only one thread executes Ruby bytecode at a time. CPU-bound work — parsing, hashing, number crunching — sees zero parallelism with Ruby threads. Four threads computing Fibonacci across 4 cores runs serially, not in parallel.

Threads still shine for I/O-bound work. When a thread blocks on a DB query or HTTP call, MRI releases the GVL, letting other threads run. That's why Puma works well for concurrent web traffic — most wall time is spent waiting on network, not executing bytecode.

Ruby 3.x introduces Ractors as the path to true CPU parallelism. Each Ractor owns its own GVL copy, runs on a dedicated OS thread, and communicates via message passing rather than shared memory. The constraint: only shareable objects cross Ractor boundaries — frozen values or objects explicitly wrapped with `Ractor.make_shareable`. Most gems remain Ractor-unsafe, limiting production adoption today.

For CPU-bound parallelism right now, `Process.fork` with `IO.pipe` or a Sidekiq-based process pool is the pragmatic path. Ractors are the future, but fork is the present.
bytes.code_snippet:
require 'benchmark'

def fib(n) = n < 2 ? n : fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2)

# Threads: GVL-bound, runs serially despite 4 OS threads
threads_time = Benchmark.realtime do
  4.times.map { Thread.new { fib(35) } }.each(&:join)
end

# Ractors: each gets its own GVL — true parallelism
ractors_time = Benchmark.realtime do
  4.times.map { Ractor.new { fib(35) } }.each(&:take)
end

puts "Threads : #{threads_time.round(2)}s"
puts "Ractors : #{ractors_time.round(2)}s"
# On a 4-core machine, Ractors run ~3-4x faster

bytes.topic: When to Choose — use cases where each language excels vs where it struggles
bytes.title: Ruby — Where It Wins and Where the GVL Will Hurt You
bytes.body:
Ruby dominates where developer velocity and expressiveness matter most: convention-driven web apps (Rails), internal tooling with rich DSLs, and script automation. Its metaprogramming — `method_missing`, `define_method`, `Module#prepend` — lets you build Rake tasks, RSpec matchers, and ActiveRecord scopes that read like prose. Sidekiq handles I/O-bound workloads (HTTP calls, DB queries) efficiently because MRI threads release the GVL during blocking I/O, so concurrency is real where it matters for web apps.

Where Ruby buckles: CPU-bound parallelism. The Global VM Lock means MRI threads cannot execute Ruby bytecode in parallel. You need `Ractor` (still maturing in 3.x) or process-level concurrency via `fork`/Puma clustered mode. Memory is another ceiling — a single Rails process routinely reaches 200–500 MB, making serverless deployments expensive. For ML inference pipelines, game servers, or high-frequency systems, reach for Go or Rust. Ruby 3.x's YJIT JIT compiler narrows the throughput gap meaningfully for request/response workloads, but raw CPU performance remains the deliberate trade-off you accept in exchange for productivity.

bytes.topic: Monitoring — runtime metrics, profiling in production, GC pauses
bytes.title: Ruby in Production — Taming GC Pauses and Runtime Metrics
bytes.body:
Ruby's GC is generational (minor/major) and can introduce latency spikes silently. `GC.stat` is your first stop — it exposes counters like `heap_live_slots`, `major_gc_count`, and `total_allocated_objects` with zero overhead. Ship these to StatsD or Prometheus on every request or via a background thread.

For wall-clock profiling in production, `stackprof` is the de facto standard. It uses `SIGPROF`-based sampling (default 1000 Hz), so overhead is negligible. Wrap hot code paths with `StackProf.run(mode: :wall, raw: true)` and dump results to a file for flamegraph analysis with `stackprof --flamegraph`.

GC pauses specifically? Enable `GC::Profiler` temporarily — not permanently — to capture per-GC elapsed time. Tune aggressiveness via env vars: `RUBY_GC_HEAP_GROWTH_FACTOR=1.1` shrinks heap growth to reduce major GC frequency. For Rails apps, `derailed_benchmarks` identifies which gems allocate most at boot. Combine `GC.compact` (Ruby 2.7+) in Puma's `before_fork` to reduce per-worker heap fragmentation.
bytes.code_snippet:
require 'stackprof'
require 'statsd-instrument'

# Sample GC stats and ship to StatsD
def report_gc_metrics
  stat = GC.stat
  StatsD.gauge('ruby.gc.heap_live_slots', stat[:heap_live_slots])
  StatsD.gauge('ruby.gc.major_gc_count', stat[:major_gc_count])
  StatsD.gauge('ruby.gc.total_allocated_objects', stat[:total_allocated_objects])
end

# Profile a specific block in production (sample 1-in-100 requests)
if rand(100) == 0
  StackProf.run(mode: :wall, raw: true, out: '/tmp/stackprof-#{Process.pid}.dump') do
    yield
  end
end
